behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is LLM non-determinism: the model could ignore context and produce generic text. Mocking the LLM at Unit or Integration level would hide this. You need a real LLM call to verify it actually incorporates specific details from the thread rather than defaulting to boilerplate.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real email thread with specific context (e.g., 'customer asks about invoice #12345 dated March 15') to the system, call the real LLM with the context-aware prompt, and verify the draft references the specific details (invoice number, date) rather than generic reply text.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real LLM to verify context incorporation; would fail or become meaningless if LLM were mocked.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: Real failure: contradictions aren't detected because LLM claim extraction is unreliable or KB lookup fails silently. In production, claim extraction likely uses an LLM to understand semantic meaning (not just keyword matching). Real LLM non-determinism determines whether contradictions are actually found. A mock LLM or regex-based extraction at Unit level would hide real extraction failures.
behaviors.test_description: Craft a draft with a specific false claim (e.g., 'Our standard delivery time is 2 days'). Populate KB with a contradictory entry ('Standard delivery is 5 business days'). Call the real fact-checker with real LLM claim extraction and KB lookup, and verify the contradiction is flagged in the output.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: LLM-based claim extraction introduces non-determinism; test requires real model to verify detection actually works.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: Real failure: LLM ignores the 'KB is empty' signal in the prompt and fabricates facts anyway. This is pure LLM non-determinism—the model's tendency to hallucinate despite constraints. Unit tests with mocked LLM would always pass (you'd mock it to include disclaimer). Only a real LLM call exposes whether the model respects the constraint or makes up facts.
behaviors.test_description: Configure an empty or deliberately irrelevant KB (no entries matching the email domain). Call the real LLM with the draft-generation prompt that includes instructions to add a disclaimer if KB is empty. Verify the generated draft contains a disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'The following is based on available information; please verify:') rather than unfounded claims.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real LLM's tendency to hallucinate vs. respect constraints is non-deterministic; mocking would mask the failure.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: Real failures are state/logic bugs: (a) approval state isn't persisted to DB, (b) the approval check is bypassed in the control flow, (c) user approval action doesn't update state. Unit tests with mocked email service would only catch (b). Integration testing with a real database and persistence layer is needed to catch (a) and (c). The behavior depends on correct state management across multiple modules, not on LLM output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft and persist it to the DB without approval flag set. Call the send pathway and verify the email service is NOT invoked. Then update the approval flag in the DB, call send again, and verify the email service IS called. Use a real database (or in-memory equivalent) to ensure approval state is correctly persisted and checked.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test explicitly checks state persistence ("create draft in DB", "update approval flag in DB"); state bugs would be missed at Unit level with mocks.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: Multiple agentic steps in sequence: (1) draft generation calls real LLM, (2) fact-checking calls real LLM again, (3) approval workflow is deterministic. Real failures involve non-determinism from multiple LLM calls or broken handoff between steps. A single Agentic test of only step 1 would miss cascading failures (e.g., fact-checker crashes on unusual LLM output, or state isn't passed correctly). Full pipeline requires Workflow level.
behaviors.test_description: End-to-end test: provide a real email thread, trigger the full pipeline (read → generate draft with real LLM → fact-check with real LLM → display approval UI). Verify the draft appears for approval without errors, then approve and verify send is triggered. Validate state transitions and data flow across all steps.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test runs multiple agentic steps (two LLM calls) in sequence and validates integration between them; any single step tested in isolation (Agentic) would miss cross-step failures.
